## Summary of US 10-Year Treasury Yield Article (Trading Economics)

**Current Data (as of March 2, 2026):**
- The 10-year US Treasury yield rose to **4.06%** on March 2, 2026, a **+0.11 percentage point** increase from the prior session
- Over the past month, the yield has **fallen by 0.21 points**
- The yield is **0.10 points lower** than a year ago

**Key Drivers Mentioned:**
- The US attacked Iranian targets and killed their supreme leader; Iran retaliated by striking civilian targets and energy infrastructure, causing shipping companies to suspend Persian Gulf trade
- This geopolitical escalation drove a **surge in energy commodity prices**, raising inflation concerns and supporting expectations of prolonged restrictive Fed policy
- The **ISM prices gauge for manufacturers** unexpectedly surged to a **three-year high**, while strong orders weakened the dovish case within the FOMC
- Markets still expect a rate cut at the **Fed's July meeting**, though rate traders increased positions for a **September cut**

**Forecasts (Trading Economics models/analysts):**
- Expected to trade at **3.95%** by end of Q1 2026
- Expected to trade at **3.74%** in 12 months

**Historical Note:**
- All-time high: **15.82%** in September 1981

> **Disclaimer:** The article appears to be a data snapshot page from Trading Economics, and some content may reflect automated or model-generated text rather than traditional editorial reporting.
</Summary>

## Summary: Yahoo Finance – ^TNX (CBOE Interest Rate 10-Year T-Note Index)

**Key Data Point (as of February 27, close):**
- **10-Year Treasury Yield (^TNX):** **3.9620%**
- **Change:** -0.0550 (-1.37%)
- **Previous Close:** 4.0170%
- **52-Week Range:** 0.0000 – 4.9970%
- **Timestamp:** February 27, at 1:59:53 PM CST

**Context from Broader Market Data:**
- The S&P 500 (^GSPC) was down 0.43%, the Dow Jones (^DJI) fell 1.05%, and the NASDAQ (^IXIC) dropped 0.92% on the same day.
- The **CBOE Volatility Index (^VIX)** rose sharply by **+6.60%** to 19.86, suggesting elevated market uncertainty on that date.
- Most major global indices were also in negative territory on February 27.

**Takeaway:** On February 27, 2026, the 10-Year Treasury yield closed at **3.962%**, down notably from the prior close of 4.017%, amid a broad equity selloff and rising volatility.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://home.treasury.gov/resource-center/data-chart-center/interest-rates/TextView?type=daily_treasury_bill_rates&field_tdr_date_value=2026">
## Summary

**Disclaimer:** This article is a methodology/reference document from the U.S. Treasury explaining how various yield curve series are constructed and published. It does **not contain any specific yield values** for the Mar 16–Mar 27, 2026 period or any recent data points relevant to forecasting.

---

### Key Points from the Article:

**Treasury Par Yield Curve (Nominal CMTs):**
- Yields are interpolated daily using a **monotone convex spline method** based on closing bid prices of the most recently auctioned Treasury securities.
- Prices are obtained by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York at approximately **3:30 PM each trading day**.
- CMT yields are read at fixed maturities: 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, and 6 months, and 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, **10**, 20, and 30 years.
- **Nominal CMT yields are floored at zero** (negative yields are not published in this series).

**Series History Notes:**
- The 1.5-month series began **February 18, 2025**.
- The 4-month series began **October 19, 2022**.
- The 2-month series began **October 16, 2018**.

**Real Yield Curve (TIPS-based R-CMTs):**
- Derived from TIPS secondary market quotations; available at 5, 7, 10, 20, and 30-year maturities.

*This document provides no actionable yield data for the resolution period in question.*
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://www.bondsavvy.com/fixed-income-investments-blog/fed-dot-plot">
## Summary: December 2025 Fed Dot Plot Analysis (Bondsavvy, December 10, 2025)

### Key Facts from the December 2025 FOMC Meeting

**Fed Funds Rate Decision:**
- The FOMC cut the fed funds target range by **25 basis points to 3.50%–3.75%** on December 10, 2025.

**December 2025 Dot Plot Projections for Year-End 2026:**
- Four participants projected **no 2026 rate cuts**
- Four participants projected **one rate cut**
- Four participants projected **two rate cuts**
- One participant (likely new appointee **Stephen Miran**) projected a year-end 2026 target range of **2.00%–2.25%** (an additional 50 bps reduction from his September projection)
- Median projections for 2026–2027 were **consistent** with the September 2025 dot plot

**FOMC Economic Projections (December 2025 SEP vs. prior):**
- Year-end 2026 **PCE inflation**: reduced by 20 bps to **2.4%**
- 2026 **real GDP growth**: increased by 50 bps to **2.3%**

### Treasury Yield Movements (Relevant to 10Y Yield)

- From **September 2024 to December 9, 2025**, the 10-year Treasury yield rose **55 basis points**
- From **September 17 to December 9, 2025** (more recent window), the 10-year yield rose only **9 basis points**
- The 10-year yield peaked in **October 2023**, fell through late 2023, rose again through spring 2024, then declined mid-April to mid-September 2024 (falling **101 bps**), before reversing upward again

### Context Note
The article does not provide a specific 10-year yield value as of December 9, 2025, but implies it is modestly above its September 17, 2025 level. No direct forecast for Q1 2026 yields is provided.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.investopedia.com/a-new-fed-cut-just-landed-but-the-2026-forecast-might-be-the-real-story-for-your-savings-11866486">
## Summary: "A New Fed Cut Just Landed—But the 2026 Forecast Might Be the Real Story for Your Savings" (Investopedia, March 2, 2026)

### Key Facts & Statistics

- The Federal Reserve delivered a **quarter-point rate cut** (the article's framing suggests this occurred at the end of 2025, described as the Fed's "third reduction of 2025" following cuts in September and October)
- Total 2025 cuts: **0.75 percentage points**, on top of **1.00 point** in cuts from late 2024
- The Fed's **2026 dot plot median projection** calls for a **single quarter-point rate cut** in 2026
- The full range of 2026 projections spans from a **0.25-point rate hike** to **1.50 points in cuts**
- Three officials projected **rate increases** in 2026; two anticipated cuts of **at least 1 percentage point**
- Best high-yield savings accounts: **4.15%–5.00% APY**
- Best nationwide CDs: up to **4.50%**, with longer-term rates of **4%+**

### Named Source Opinions

- **Fed Chair Jerome Powell** acknowledged the dual-risk dilemma: *"A very large number of participants agree that risks are to the upside for unemployment and to the upside for inflation… You've got one tool. You can't do two things at once."*

### Additional Context

- Government data delays stemming from a recent shutdown were noted as adding further uncertainty to Fed projections
- Deposit rates are expected to **drift modestly lower** following the cut, but not drop sharply

> **Disclaimer:** The article conflates timeline references (describing the cut as both a "year-end 2025" move and referencing a March 2026 publication date), which may indicate editorial inconsistency or repurposed content. Readers should interpret the rate environment figures with that caveat in mind.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/10/fed-interest-rate-decision-december-2025-.html">
## Summary: Fed Approves Third Rate Cut of 2025, Signals Caution Ahead

**Source:** CNBC | **Date:** December 10, 2025 | **Author:** Jeff Cox

### Key Decision
The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut its benchmark overnight borrowing rate by **0.25 percentage points**, placing it in a range of **3.5%–3.75%**. This was characterized as a **"hawkish cut"** — a reduction paired with cautious signals about future policy.

### Dissents (9-3 Vote)
The most divided Fed vote since September 2019:
- **Governor Stephen Miran** (dovish dissent): favored a larger 0.50 pp cut
- **Presidents Jeffrey Schmid & Austan Goolsbee** (hawkish dissents): preferred holding rates steady

### Forward Guidance
- The FOMC reused language from **December 2024** that previously signaled a pause in cuts — after which no reductions occurred until **September 2025**
- Chair Powell stated: *"We are well positioned to wait and see how the economy evolves"*

### Economic Projections
- **GDP growth for 2026** revised upward by 0.5 pp to **2.3%**
- **Inflation** expected to remain above the 2% target **until 2028**; current preferred gauge at **2.8%** (as of September 2025)

### Balance Sheet
- Fed announced it will **resume buying Treasury securities**, starting with **$40 billion in Treasury bills**, following a halt to balance sheet runoff
- Purchases expected to remain elevated "for a few months" before being "significantly reduced"

### Market Reaction
- **Stocks rose** (~500 points on the Dow)
- **Treasury yields moved mostly lower**

### Political Context
- Powell has **three meetings remaining** before his term ends
- President Trump signaled he will prioritize **lower-rate preference** in selecting a new Fed chair
- Prediction markets (Kalshi) gave **Kevin Hassett 72% odds** of being nominated

### Labor Market Note
- Data described as a **"low-hire, low-fire"** environment
- Announced layoffs through November exceeded **1.1 million**, per Challenger, Gray & Christmas
- Data availability was hampered by a ~**six-week government shutdown** ending November 12
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://economics.td.com/us-cpi">
## Summary: U.S. CPI Report – January 2026 (TD Economics, Feb 13, 2026)

**Source:** TD Economics, authored by Thomas Feltmate (Director & Senior Economist). This is a named, institutional source with reasonable reliability.

---

### Key Data Points

- **Headline CPI:** +0.2% month-on-month (m/m) in January 2026 — one-tenth **below** Bloomberg consensus forecast
- **Year-on-year CPI:** **2.4%**, down from 2.7% in December 2025
- **Energy:** -1.5% m/m (gasoline: -3.2% m/m)
- **Food prices:** +0.2% m/m (down from +0.7% in December)
- **Core CPI (ex-food & energy):** +0.3% m/m (above the prior 12-month average of +0.2%); year-on-year at **2.5%**, down from 2.6%
- **Services inflation:** +0.4% m/m — fastest monthly gain since July 2025; airfares surged +6.5% m/m
- **Primary shelter:** +0.2% m/m (cooler than expected)
- **Core goods:** flat for second consecutive month, dragged down by used vehicle prices (-1.8% m/m)

---

### Key Implications (per TD Economics)

- Report came in **slightly cooler than expected**, but forward-looking risks are **tilted to the upside**
- **Tariff pass-through** expected to firm core goods inflation in coming months
- **Demand-side risks** include potential tax cuts (OBBBA), easier financial conditions, and a stabilizing labor market
- TD Economics expects the **Fed to remain on hold until at least summer 2026**

---

*Note: This article focuses on January 2026 CPI data and does not directly reference the 10-Year Treasury yield. Its relevance to the question is indirect — inflation expectations and Fed policy outlook are key drivers of the 10Y yield.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.capitaleconomics.com/publications/global-inflation-watch/global-inflation-undershoot-consensus-forecasts">
## Summary

**Source:** Capital Economics | **Date:** February 3, 2026
**Note:** This is a paywalled article; only the preview/abstract is available. The full content could not be accessed.

### Key Points from Available Content:

**Global Inflation Outlook:**
- Capital Economics expects global inflation to **undershoot consensus forecasts**, driven by three main factors:
  1. **Falling commodity prices**
  2. **Cooling labour markets**
  3. **Absence of significant inflationary spillovers from tariffs**

**Country/Region-Specific Views:**
- **United States:** Core inflation expected to **ease later in 2026** once the initial boost from tariffs fades, with additional disinflationary pressure from AI-powered productivity gains
- **Euro-zone & UK:** Softer wage growth anticipated to **reduce services inflation** from currently elevated levels
- **China:** Recent uptick in inflation expected to **give way to renewed persistent deflation**

**Broader Assessment:**
- Inflation in most major economies projected to be **within 1 percentage point of central bank targets** in 2026
- Inflation skewed **below target in Asia** and **above target in emerging Europe**

### Relevance to UST 10Y Yield Question:
Lower-than-expected inflation could reduce upward pressure on Treasury yields, potentially supporting lower 10Y yields in the Mar 16–27 period.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/12/the-january-cpi-inflation-report-is-due-out-friday-morning-heres-what-its-expected-to-show.html">
## Summary: January CPI Inflation Report Preview (CNBC, February 12, 2026)

### Key Expectations
- **Headline CPI (January):** Expected at **2.5% year-over-year** (Dow Jones consensus), down from 2.7% in December
- **Core CPI (ex-food & energy):** Was 2.6% in December; both headline and core expected to show **0.3% monthly increases**
- **Goldman Sachs forecast:** Slightly lighter headline CPI at **2.4%**, with tariffs contributing ~0.07 percentage points to core inflation

### Recent Trend Context
- CPI has been on a **downward trajectory** since peaking just above 3% in September 2025
- CPI has **come in below Wall Street consensus for the past three months**
- A 2.5% reading would match **May 2025 levels** — shortly after "Liberation Day" tariffs were enacted

### Fed Policy Implications
- Fed funds rate currently targeted at **3.5%–3.75%**
- A soft CPI reading could give the Fed **more confidence to cut rates**
- Tom Lee (Fundstrat): Fed "has a lot of room to cut" given rates remain well above pre-Covid levels

### Market Context
- Markets pulled back after a **strong January jobs report** (130,000 nonfarm payrolls; unemployment at 4.3%), raising concerns the Fed might delay cuts
- A soft CPI reading could **offset those concerns**, per analysts
</Summary>
